slides.title: Data with Labels, Not Positions
slides.body: A **dictionary** stores information as `key: value` pairs. Instead of remembering *position 0*, you look things up by a meaningful **name** — just like a real dictionary.
slides.tip: Dictionaries are Python's go-to structure for any data that has labels — settings, profiles, scores, and more.

slides.title: Create Your First Dictionary
slides.body: Wrap `key: value` pairs in `{}`, separated by commas. Keys are usually strings; values can be anything.
slides.tip: Use square brackets `d[key]` to read a value — like indexing, but with a name instead of a number.

slides.title: Safe Lookups with `.get()`
slides.body: Accessing a key that does not exist raises a `KeyError`. Use `.get(key, default)` to return a fallback value instead of crashing.
slides.tip: When you are not sure a key exists, `.get()` is always the safer choice.

slides.title: Add, Update, and Remove
slides.body: Assign `d[key] = value` to **add** a new entry or **overwrite** an existing one. Use `del d[key]` to permanently remove it.
slides.code:
scores = {"Alice": 90, "Bob": 75}
scores["Charlie"] = 88
scores["Bob"] = 82
del scores["Alice"]
print(scores)
slides.tip: Same syntax for adding and updating — Python handles both automatically.

slides.title: Loop with `.items()`
slides.body: Use `.items()` to loop over every key-value pair at once. It hands you both pieces together on each iteration.
slides.tip: Also useful: `.keys()` for keys only, `.values()` for values only.

slides.title: You've Got Dictionaries!
slides.body: You can now create, read, update, delete, and loop over dictionaries — the most powerful built-in data structure in Python. Nested dicts and dict comprehensions are your next power-ups.
slides.tip: Keys must be immutable (strings, numbers, tuples) and unique. Lists cannot be keys.
